quote: Mary recognizes Jesus when he says, "Mary!" and she clings to him.
firstReading: Peter proclaims that God has made Jesus both Lord and Messiah and calls the people to repent and be baptized in his name for the forgiveness of sins. He promises the gift of the Holy Spirit to those who respond. Many accept the message, are baptized, and about three thousand are added that day, marking the rapid growth of the early Church and the communal consequences of apostolic proclamation.
psalmSummary: The psalm praises the faithful and upright character of the Lord’s word and his love of righteousness and justice. It affirms that the eyes of the Lord watch over those who fear him and deliver them from death and distress. The community expresses trust: their soul waits for the Lord and they hope in his steadfast love, concluding with a plea for God’s mercy to rest upon them.
gospelSummary: Mary Magdalene stands weeping outside the empty tomb, looks inside and sees the angels, then turns and sees Jesus but does not at first recognize him. When Jesus speaks her name she recognizes him and addresses him as Teacher. He commissions her not to hold him as before but to go and tell the disciples that he is ascending to the Father. Mary becomes the first messenger of the resurrection, sent to announce that she has seen the Lord.
saintReflection: St. John Baptist de La Salle (Memorial): St. John Baptist de La Salle (1651-1719) was a French priest and educational reformer who founded the Institute of the Brothers of the Christian Schools (De La Salle Brothers). He revolutionized education by teaching in the vernacular rather than Latin, grouping students by ability, and making high-quality education accessible to the poor. He is the patron saint of teachers.
dailyPrayer: Risen Lord, you call us by name and send us to share the good news. Give us ears to hear your voice and hearts brave enough to respond as Peter urged: to repent, be baptized, and receive your Spirit. Strengthen teachers and mentors following St. John Baptist de La Salle’s witness, that they may make the Gospel intelligible and accessible to every child, especially the poor. When we weep at loss or fail to recognize you in our midst, speak our name and restore our sight. Grant us courage to obey your commission to go and tell, so our lives draw others to faith. Bless pastors, catechists, and families who form faith at home and school. May your steadfast love sustain us; may your mercy rest upon your Church and the world. Send your Spirit to enliven our witness and unite us in mission. Through Christ our Lord. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: The readings connect apostolic preaching, trusting praise, and an intimate resurrection encounter into a single Easter claim: Christ is Lord, calling us to conversion and mission. Peter’s word establishes the communal response of repentance, baptism, and reception of the Spirit that forms the Church. The psalm provides the theological ground of trust in God’s faithful providence. John’s Gospel makes the event personal: recognition of the risen Lord comes when he names us, and that encounter becomes the basis for commissioning. St. John Baptist de La Salle’s missionary-educational work models how proclamation, formation, and service advance the Church’s mission—teaching people to recognize, follow, and proclaim the Lord.
exegesis:
Context: The Acts passage stands at the climax of Peter’s Pentecost proclamation, where he interprets Jesus’ death and exaltation as the decisive fulfillment of God’s plan and insists on repentance, baptism, and the gift of the Spirit as the appropriate communal responses. That sermon functions as both theology and pastoral summons: it diagnoses the crowd’s culpability, announces God’s saving act, and prescribes sacramental incorporation into a renewed people of God. The numerical result—about three thousand—signals the movement from proclamation to an embodied, social community that evidences the Spirit’s presence and creates the Church’s initial structure and identity.

Psalm: The selected verses of Psalm 33 praise the reliability of God’s word and his just care for the world. The psalmist affirms that the Lord’s ways are upright, and that God’s attention rests particularly upon those who fear him, delivering them from death and trouble. In the context of Acts’ call to repentance and baptism, the psalm supplies the spiritual atmosphere of trust in divine faithfulness: conversion is not a gamble but an entrance into God’s dependable care. The concluding petition for mercy ties personal trust to communal intercession, reminding believers that new life involves dependence on God’s steadfast love.

Gospel: John 20:11–18 stages an intimate resurrection encounter. Mary’s grief and failure to recognize Jesus at first highlight both human sorrow and transformed perception: recognition occurs when the risen Lord addresses her by name. That personal address functions as ontological revelation—the risen One restores relationship and identity. Jesus’ command not to hold him and his commissioning of Mary to tell the disciples reveal the new mode of presence and mission after the resurrection: he sends, and his followers become heralds. Mary’s role as the first witness underscores the Gospel’s subversion of expected lines of authority and the priority of personal testimony in transmission of the resurrection.

Saints: St. John Baptist de La Salle brings the readings into ecclesial practice. His reforms aimed to make religious truth accessible, to form communal structures for learning, and to equip lay and religious teachers to serve the poor. The connection to today’s texts is practical: proclamation (Acts), trustful formation (Psalm), and personal encounter leading to mission (John) all require systems of education and ongoing formation. De La Salle’s work models how the Church incarnates apostolic commissioning in institutions: schools become places where people are called by name, instructed in faith, and prepared to witness.

Theology: Together these texts shape a theology of encounter that moves from call to incorporation to commission. The Spirit-empowered community responds to apostolic naming of Jesus as Lord by ritual reception and ethical reorientation. The psalm assures believers that such reorientation rests on God’s faithful governance. John’s narrative stresses that encounter with the risen Christ is personal and vocational: being named by him effects recognition and sends the believer out. The implication is ecclesial: true catechesis, liturgy, and pastoral care must foster hearing, recognition, and sending.

Today: For contemporary Christians the passages pose practical questions: where are we called to repent and be baptized anew, and how do we form communities that both trust God and carry the Gospel to others? Educators, catechists, and pastors are asked to follow de La Salle’s example by making the faith intelligible and accessible, especially to the marginalized. Mary’s witness invites the baptized to testify from personal encounter rather than merely abstract assent. The Church’s mission remains to name Jesus as Lord in word and deed, to cultivate trust in God’s care, and to equip people to recognize, follow, and announce the risen Lord in everyday contexts.
